SDG 9

KHAS actively promotes innovation and infrastructure development through its interdisciplinary research projects, technology-focused curricula, and collaborative initiatives. Engineering and design programs focus on sustainable construction, smart cities, and digital transformation in industry. Research centers and laboratories support projects in energy systems, industrial design, and urban mobility, contributing to Turkey’s innovation ecosystem. Partnerships with Teknopark Silivri and external institutions provide students and faculty with opportunities to pilot solutions that strengthen industrial competitiveness and advance sustainable infrastructure.

9.2. Projects

Academic Open-Access Repository (2023): Continuous improvements were made to the repository at https://academicrepository.khas.edu.tr/, which received 98,853 visits from 86 countries. A total of 2,858 records were downloaded 93,185 times.

Academic Open-Access Repository (2024): Migrated to new software at https://gcris.khas.edu.tr/, hosting 5,166 items accessed by 204,123 visitors from 181 countries, with 174,031 downloads.

Read-and-Publish Agreements: Agreements with Wiley, SAGE, Cambridge University Press, and others; 43 out of 214 KHAS-affiliated articles were published as open access under these agreements.

National Cultural Documentation: Phase 1 of the Reşat Ekrem Koçu Istanbul Encyclopaedia web project launched at https://istanbulansiklopedisi.org/.

TÜBİTAK 1001 Project No. 221K006 – “The Role of Risk-Factor Knowledge in Individual Investors’ Preference for Unregulated Financial Products: Developing a Map of Unregulated Financial Products for Turkey, Assessing Regulatory Impacts, and Proposing Legal Frameworks” | Project Leader: Assoc. Prof. Dr. Esra Hamamcıoğlu | Researchers: Asst. Prof. Dr. Alara Efsun Yazıcıoğlu, Asst. Prof. Dr. Argun Karamanlıoğlu, Asst. Prof. Dr. Aslı Togan Eğrican, Dr. Davut Gürses, Asst. Prof. Dr. Murat Tiniç

[13]

Description: In Turkey, individual investments in unregulated or informal markets and resulting financial losses remain a persistent issue. A key reason for risky, irrational investment behavior is lack of knowledge about the risks of financial products, compounded by low financial and legal literacy, which negatively affect risk perception. Understanding why unregulated investments remain widespread despite existing laws requires an interdisciplinary analysis of individuals’ risk perception, combining law, finance, and educational design.

Occupational Health and Safety (ISG) Projects:

The Technopark aims to contribute to the regional and national economy through R&D projects carried out under the University-Public-Industry-Civil Society quadruple-helix model.

FabriKHAS acts with the vision of becoming a global creativity hub in the screen industries, aiming to lead innovative projects by connecting local talent to international platforms.

TÜBİTAK 1001 Project – Prof. Dr. Baysal – Digital Personality and Posthumous Data Protection in the Information Society

This project examines how posthumous digital identity is handled under Turkish law, individuals’ attitudes and risk perceptions, and evaluates the adequacy of the current legal framework. Conducted over 36 months with a multidisciplinary team in law, informatics, psychology, and design, the project involves comparative research with French, German, and Anglo-American legal systems. It uses qualitative and quantitative methods to analyze psychological and legal issues caused by information asymmetry, ultimately aiming to develop legal policies and propose regulations for protecting personal data after death.

TÜBİTAK 3005 Project – Asst. Prof. Dr. Gizem Arslan – Comparison of Turkey’s Urban Transformation Legislation for Climate Change Mitigation with EU Law and the French Example, and Identification of Necessary Legal Amendments

The project focuses on the role and impact of urban transformation and renewal practices in combating climate change. It aims to provide a legal and scientific foundation for Turkey’s climate policy by conducting a comparative legal analysis of European Union regulations that strengthen legal frameworks for climate action.

TÜBİTAK 1001 Project – Assoc. Prof. Dr. Esra Hamamcıoğlu – The Role of Risk-Factor Knowledge in Individual Investors’ Preference for Unregulated Financial Products: Developing a Map of Unregulated Financial Products for Turkey, Assessing Regulatory Impacts, and Proposing Legal Frameworks

This project investigates the underlying reasons for individuals’ high-risk investment behavior in unregulated and informal markets in Turkey, emphasizing the impact of knowledge gaps and low risk perception. Combining law, finance, and educational design, the project uses surveys, training programs, and controlled experiments to analyze how risk perception influences investment decisions. It also evaluates the adequacy of current regulations and develops user-friendly guides and policy recommendations.

9.4. Courses

LAW 433 – Information Technology Law (Elective)

Course Description: Covers the hybrid structure of IT law and contemporary, debated topics including crypto assets, unfair competition on the internet, the legal dimension of artificial intelligence, and smart contracts.

[14]

LAW 436 – Capital Markets Law (Elective)

Course Description: Capital markets, as a component of financial markets, are strictly regulated and supervised both globally and in Turkey. The main objectives are to ensure market efficiency, provide corporate financing, and effectively protect investor rights. This course examines Capital Markets Law, the secondary legislation issued by the Capital Markets Board, and the fundamental principles established to achieve these goals.

[15]

LAW 455 – Algorithms, Rights, and Liabilities (Elective)

Course Description: This course explores the impact of artificial intelligence and algorithms on criminal, contract, and labor law processes, providing students with a comprehensive understanding of the challenges posed to these legal areas by the digital future.

[16]

LAW 460 – Financial Technologies and Law (Elective)

Course Description: Focuses on the relationship between emerging financial technologies and the law, as well as the application of existing legal frameworks to these new fields. Using case-based learning, the course prepares students for legal issues they may encounter in practice, while improving teamwork and presentation skills.

[17]

LAW 462 – Legal Design (Elective)

Course Description: 1) Introduction to the concept and significance of legal design; 2) Fundamentals and principles of design; 3) Methods for applying legal design in different areas of law; 4) Conducting sample legal design exercises.

[18]

LAW 471 – Artificial Intelligence and Work Life (Elective)

Course Description: Examines the impact of artificial intelligence and algorithms on labor law processes, providing students with a comprehensive understanding of the challenges posed to labor law by future digital environments.

[19]

LAW 472 – Neuroscience and Law (Elective)

Course Description: Modern legal systems often assume that humans are conscious beings with free will, which is a foundational concept for legal personality and responsibility. Legal accountability requires that actions and transactions are carried out knowingly and voluntarily. However, since the 1980s, developments in neuroscience have increasingly challenged this assumption. This course examines whether advances in neuroscience might require future legal systems to rethink the basis of legal responsibility.

[20]

KHAS 1738 – Protection of Personal Data in the Digital Age (Core Curriculum)

Course Description: Systematic exploration of regulations and court cases in personal data protection law, including: definition and scope of personal data, cookies and data tracking, BigTech (GAFAM) data processing, consent and its validity, the right to be forgotten, big data, post-mortem personal data protection and digital inheritance, the economic value of personal data in contracts, and the benefits and risks of deepfakes.
